sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) delivers positive airway pressure via a tight-fitting mask without endotracheal intubation. Prehospital CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure) and BiPAP (Bilevel Positive Airway Pressure) improve oxygenation and reduce work of breathing in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, severe asthma, and COPD exacerbations.

sections.title: Indications & Contraindications
sections.content:
**Indications:**
- Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (ACPO) — strong evidence
- Acute COPD exacerbation with type 2 respiratory failure
- Severe asthma (not responding to bronchodilators)
- Hypoxic respiratory failure (SpO₂ < 90% despite high-flow O₂)

**Contraindications:**
- Apnoea or respiratory arrest (use BVM)
- Unable to protect airway / GCS ≤ 8
- Vomiting / high aspiration risk
- Facial trauma precluding mask fit
- Undrained tension pneumothorax
- Haemodynamic instability (relative)

sections.title: Setup & Application
sections.content:
**CPAP setup:**

1. Select appropriate mask size (full-face preferred for better seal)

2. Explain procedure to patient — cooperation is essential

3. Set CPAP pressure: start at 5–7.5 cmH₂O; titrate up to 10–12.5 cmH₂O if needed

4. Connect oxygen (typically 15 L/min or per device flow requirement)

5. Apply mask with patient holding initially, then secure harness

6. Monitor: SpO₂, respiratory rate, work of breathing, patient tolerance

7. Titrate to SpO₂ ≥ 94% and reduced work of breathing

**BiPAP typical settings (per local protocol):**
- IPAP: 10–15 cmH₂O
- EPAP: 4–5 cmH₂O

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Early application improves outcomes in ACPO — do not delay
- Reassess every 5–10 minutes; deterioration may require RSI
- Gastric insufflation is a risk — have suction immediately available
- Remove mask briefly if patient vomits
- Document: initial SpO₂, RR, work of breathing, pressure used, and response
